ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for filled curry bun; closest parent bread product term FOODON_03315671 used as nearest defensible anchor. A more specific filled-bun term does not exist in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_description: Filled bread / stuffed bun — soft wheat bread roll with savoury filling
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP (filled bread) used as closest parent category for a soft bun filled with curry chicken and potato. No more specific FoodEx2 term for mamak-style curry bun exists in the current release.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT food concept identified for chicken curry bun; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard chicken curry bun weighs approximately 100 g and is typically consumed as a single snack or light meal item.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Curry Bun — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: All core macros and secondary nutrients sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo entry for Chicken Curry Bun (F05020025) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for similar filled wheat bun with curry chicken filling as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown separately.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken curry bun in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 70 (high) based on the closest analogous food: white wheat bread roll / soft white bun (GI ~70–72, University of Sydney GI Database). The refined white flour bun dominates the carbohydrate fraction (34.99 g/100 g); the curry chicken and potato filling contributes modest protein and fat that slightly attenuate but do not substantially lower the overall GI. Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (70 × 34.99) / 100 = 24.5, classified as high GL (≥20).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 414 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold for hypertension concern, with no meaningful potassium or dietary fibre offset.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit chicken curry bun to half a serving (≈50 g) per occasion, avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods, and request reduced-salt curry filling where possible, as a single 100 g bun already delivers approximately 18% of the 2300 mg daily sodium limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI refined white flour bun (GI ~70) with 35 g/100 g total carbohydrate, 9.6 g/100 g sugar, and only 2.1 g/100 g dietary fibre produces a high glycaemic load of 24.5 per 100 g serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit chicken curry bun due to its high glycaemic load; if consumed, restrict to half a bun (≈50 g), pair with non-starchy vegetables and a protein source to blunt the post-meal glucose spike, and choose wholemeal bun variants when available.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat 2.8 g/100 g and trans fat ~0.1 g/100 g from shortening or butter in the dough, combined with high sodium (414 mg/100 g), raise cardiovascular risk when consumed regularly.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, chicken curry bun should be an occasional food only; the combination of saturated fat, estimated trans fat, and high sodium exceeds recommended limits for daily intake, and the deep-fried variant should be avoided entirely due to substantially higher total and saturated fat content.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined white flour promotes glycaemic-driven inflammation; estimated trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) is a pro-inflammatory disqualifier; potato in filling is a nightshade that may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid chicken curry bun due to its refined carbohydrate base, estimated trans fat content, and nightshade potato filling, all of which may exacerbate inflammatory activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken curry bun is a popular Singaporean mamak-style snack consisting of a soft, lightly sweetened white wheat flour bread roll filled with a spiced curry chicken and potato mixture. The bun is typically baked until golden and has a pillowy, slightly chewy texture with a moist, aromatic filling flavoured with curry powder, turmeric, cumin, coriander, chilli, and onion. It is widely sold at mamak stalls, Indian Muslim bakeries, and hawker centres across Singapore, and is commonly eaten as a breakfast item or mid-morning snack. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 267 kcal, 9.2 g protein, 9.95 g fat, and 35 g carbohydrate with 9.6 g sugar and only 2.1 g dietary fibre. Sodium is high at 414 mg/100 g. The refined white flour base gives the bun a high glycaemic index (~70) and a high glycaemic load (~24.5 per 100 g), making it unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or hypertension without strict portion control. The estimated trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) from shortening in the dough and the nightshade potato filling also make it unsuitable for rheumatoid arthritis management. It is halal-certified and gluten-containing. A standard serving is approximately one bun (100 g).
common_user_queries: How many calories in a chicken curry bun?
Is curry bun high in sodium?
What is the GI of chicken curry bun?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F05020025 with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy analogue for similar filled wheat bun and documented in notes. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous white bread roll entry with full documentation. All mandatory multilingual names present. Ontology anchors are defensible with correct match types.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for chicken curry bun is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific composite dish as of 2026-04-16. potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite mamak-style bun are not individually reported in HPB Foodo F05020025 or in a directly matching USDA FoodData Central entry as of 2026-04-16. trans_fat_g set to 0.1 as an estimate based on typical shortening or butter content in commercial soft bun dough; this is an estimated value and not directly reported by HPB. saturated_fat_g of 2.8 g/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy analogue (soft wheat roll with savoury filling) as HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this entry. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on white wheat bread roll / soft white bun from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for chicken curry bun exists in the database. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as (70 × 34.99) / 100 = 24.5 g for a 100 g serving. Ontology: FoodOn match type set to closest_parent (FOODON_03315671, bread product) and FoodEx2 match type set to closest_parent (A0FKP, filled bread) as these are the nearest defensible parent categories; no exact-match terms exist for mamak-style curry bun in either ontology as of 2026-04-16.
